_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Washington County, NY. Formed 1772 from Albany County (originally as Charlotte County, renamed 1784). No major fire. Strong New England-pattern record-keeping. Quaker meeting records are a key substitute source.
display_name: Washington County, New York — Courthouse Atlas

current_courthouse.location: Fort Edward, NY — Upper Broadway (county seat shared administratively with Hudson Falls)

previous_courthouses.location: Various locations — county disrupted by Revolutionary War frontier warfare
previous_courthouses.fate: The county was originally named Charlotte County (for the Queen). Renamed Washington County in 1784. Revolutionary War frontier raids from Canada disrupted civil administration.

previous_courthouses.location: Salem, then Fort Edward (county seat moved 1847)
previous_courthouses.fate: Frame and stone courthouses. No significant fire damage.

record_losses.cause: Revolutionary War frontier warfare disrupted record-keeping
records_destroyed: Some scattered early papers may have been lost during raids and evacuations
records_survived: Most deed books and court records survived the war period
record_losses.description: Washington County (then Charlotte County) was on the frontier during the Revolution. British and Loyalist raids from Canada drove settlers south, and civil administration was disrupted for several years. Most recorded volumes survived, but some loose papers and early minutes may have been lost. The county clerk's office was not specifically targeted.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} lived in Washington County during the Revolution, records from the 1777-1783 period may be incomplete — not from fire but from the chaos of frontier warfare that scattered families and disrupted government.
record_losses.source: Stone, William L. Washington County, New York: Its History to the Close of the Nineteenth Century. New York: New York History Company, 1901.

deeds.completeness: Good from near the county's formation. The earliest volumes show some gaps from Revolutionary War disruption.
deeds.repository: Washington County Clerk's Office, Fort Edward; NY State Archives

wills_estates.repository: Washington County Surrogate's Court; NY State Archives

marriages.gaps: Pre-1880 civil registration sparse. Quaker and Congregational church records fill the gap.
marriages.repository: Town clerks; NY State Department of Health

court_minutes.completeness: Fair — Revolutionary War era has gaps.
court_minutes.repository: Washington County Clerk's Office; NY State Archives

vital_records.repository: Town clerks; NY State Department of Health

substitute_sources.description: The Society of Friends maintained meetings in Washington County from the 1770s. Meeting records include birth, marriage, death, removal certificates, and disciplinary minutes.
substitute_sources.repository: Haviland Records Room, New York Yearly Meeting; Swarthmore College Friends Historical Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Exceptional. Quaker removal certificates document migration with precision — stating where a family came from and where they went. Hinshaw's Encyclopedia abstracts many Washington County entries.
substitute_sources.source: Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.

substitute_sources.description: Washington County settlers from New England brought town-meeting governance. Town records include vital statistics, road records, poor relief, and militia lists from the late 18th century.
substitute_sources.repository: Washington County town clerks; NY State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable. New England-pattern town records are more detailed than typical NY town records because the settlers brought their record-keeping traditions.
substitute_sources.source: Stone, William L. Washington County, New York. New York History Company, 1901.

substitute_sources.description: Canal construction and operation records from the 1820s onward.
substitute_sources.repository: NY State Archives; Washington County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Useful for identifying canal workers and their families.
substitute_sources.source: Whitford, Noble E. History of the Canal System of the State of New York. Albany: State of New York, 1906.

narrative_hooks: Washington County was originally Charlotte County — named for the Queen — and renamed after the Revolution. {ancestor_name}'s pre-1784 records are filed under 'Charlotte County' in the earliest volumes.

Quaker meeting records in Washington County are among the most genealogically valuable sources in the region — {ancestor_name}'s removal certificate documents where the family came from and where they went.

Revolutionary War frontier raids disrupted Washington County's civil administration — {ancestor_name}'s records from the 1777-1783 period may be incomplete because the government could barely function while the county was a war zone.

Washington County's New England settlers brought New England record-keeping — town records here are more detailed than in most NY counties because the Yankees who settled the county insisted on documenting everything.
source_references: Stone, William L. Washington County, New York: Its History to the Close of the Nineteenth Century. New York: New York History Company, 1901.
Hinshaw, William Wade. Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy. Ann Arbor: Edwards Brothers, 1936.
Whitford, Noble E. History of the Canal System of the State of New York. Albany: State of New York, 1906.
New York State Archives. Guide to Records in the New York State Archives. Albany, 1993.
